summary:
Neoplatonism (founded c. 3rd century AD by Plotinus in Alexandria and Rome) was the last great philosophical system of pagan antiquity — a sophisticated metaphysical and mystical philosophy that synthesized Plato's ideas with Pythagorean and Stoic elements into a comprehensive account of reality, knowledge, and the soul's journey. Plotinus (204–270 AD), who studied in Alexandria before moving to Rome and attracting a circle of aristocratic students, produced the Enneads (edited by his student Porphyry) — arguably the most profound philosophical work of the ancient world after Plato and Aristotle. His system posited a hierarchical emanation of reality from the supreme principle 'the One,' through 'Intellect' (nous) and 'Soul,' down to matter.

Neoplatonism's influence on subsequent thought was immense and paradoxical: though a pagan philosophy, it became the intellectual framework through which Christian theologians understood and expressed Christian doctrine. Augustine of Hippo's theology was deeply shaped by Neoplatonic concepts; Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite applied Neoplatonic hierarchy to Christian angels and the mystical ascent to God; and Neoplatonic metaphysics underpinned much of medieval Christian scholasticism. In the Islamic world, Neoplatonic texts translated into Arabic (often attributed to Aristotle) profoundly shaped Islamic philosophy. The Academy at Athens — where later Neoplatonists like Proclus taught — was closed by Emperor Justinian in 529 AD, conventionally marking the end of ancient philosophy, though Neoplatonic ideas continued in medieval Christian and Islamic thought.
